answer:
**The work *San Hyan Tattvajnana* (a Medieval Kawi prose text in the Śaiva tradition, blending Śaiva and Sāṃkhya concepts) discusses Pradhānatattva (primordial nature/matter, child of Māyātattva, characterized by forgetfulness akin to sleep) in relation to Śiva (here termed the Sovereign Lord or *Īśa*/Īśvara, the ultimate director of creation).**[^1]

**The Sovereign Lord (Śiva) initiates creation by willing the Divine Ātmā (soul/Puruṣa) to unite with Pradhānatattva, animating it and producing *citta* (mind) and the *triguṇa* (three qualities: *sattva*, *rajas*, *tamas*), which drive saṃsāra.**[^2]

**The specific verse metaphorizing this relation appears toward the end (¶¶ 27–28), likening Pradhāna to a cart, the Ātmā/Puruṣa to an ox, and Īśa (Śiva) to the charioteer who directs the turning wheel of the world (saṃsāra):**[^3]

> *śakaṭopamaṃ pradhānaṃ puruṣo vṛṣabhopamah /*  
> *īśasārathisaṃyuktaṃ jagat bhramitacakravat //*

**Translation:** "Pradhānatattva is likened to a cart. The ātmā [Puruṣa] is likened to an ox. Īśa [Lord Śiva], as charioteer, is joined [to them]; the world turns like a mill-wheel."

**This embedded śloka (in the original Kawi/Sanskrit) encapsulates Śiva's directive role over Pradhāna and Puruṣa in cosmic manifestation and rebirth.**[^3]

[^1]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, ch. 1 [full chapter](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/).

[^2]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, ch. 1, ¶6 ([p-ef14cc](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-ef14cc)).

[^3]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, ch. 1, ¶¶27–28 (verses at [p-a9c453](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-a9c453) and [p-e48a3e](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-e48a3e)).
